questions.prompt: Why is `PasswordAuthentication no` + key-only SSH a baseline practice?

choices.text: Internet-facing SSH on port 22 receives constant brute-force attempts — automated scripts try millions of common username/password combinations against every responsive host. With password auth enabled, weak passwords get cracked eventually. With keys-only, the same attempts hit no possible match (the server doesn't accept passwords at all); the attacks become noise instead of risk. Compound with `PermitRootLogin no` (so even compromised non-root accounts can't directly take over) and `AllowUsers` (explicit allowlist of who can SSH at all). This combination blocks the vast majority of SSH attacks — they're brute force, brute force assumes password auth, password auth is disabled. Use SSH keys with passphrases stored in agent (ssh-agent + macOS Keychain or equivalent), and you're protected against both online and offline attacks.

questions.explanation: Brute-force attempts assume password auth. Keys-only renders the entire attack class harmless.

questions.prompt: You're running Docker containers and want defense-in-depth against container breakouts. Practices?

choices.text: Run all as root for simplicity.

Multiple layers: (1) run containers as a non-root user (USER directive in Dockerfile; or `--user 1000` at runtime) — exploits can't easily escalate, (2) read-only root filesystem (`--read-only`) — even if exploited, no persistent changes, (3) drop unneeded Linux capabilities (`--cap-drop=ALL --cap-add=NET_BIND_SERVICE` if you need <1024 ports) — most apps don't need most caps, (4) use seccomp profiles to block dangerous syscalls (Docker's default is reasonable), (5) NEVER mount /var/run/docker.sock into the container — that gives full host control to anyone in the container, (6) keep the host kernel patched (container isolation depends on kernel features that have had escape CVEs), (7) scan images with Trivy/Snyk for known-vuln packages. Layers compound: if one breaks, others limit damage. Default-docker (root user, all caps, read-write) is a single failure away from host compromise.

questions.explanation: Non-root user + read-only fs + drop caps + seccomp + never mount docker.sock + patched host + image scanning.
